title: Moving to Twickenham: A Guide to Living There
meta: Moving to Twickenham? Our local guide covers the area, transport, schools, riverside life and tips for a smooth move from a Sussex removals firm.
h1: Moving to Twickenham: A Guide to Living in Twickenham
lead: Moving to Twickenham puts you in one of south-west London's most appealing riverside neighbourhoods, a leafy, village-flavoured corner of the borough of Richmond upon Thames that somehow manages to feel both connected and calm. Famous worldwide as the home of English rugby, Twickenham is far more than its stadium: it is a place of riverside walks, independent shops, good schools and a genuine sense of community. This guide walks through what living in Twickenham is really like, and how to make your move there as smooth as possible.
body:
Where Is Twickenham, and Why Do People Love It?

Twickenham sits on the north bank of the River Thames in the London Borough of Richmond upon Thames, roughly ten miles south-west of central London. It enjoys that rare combination of being properly part of London while feeling like a riverside town with its own distinct character. The Thames defines much of its appeal, with the water never far away and several of the loveliest stretches of the river within easy reach.

People are drawn to Twickenham for the balance it strikes. You get green space, river views and a relaxed pace, yet a fast train into Waterloo and the buzz of central London is never far off. Add a strong line-up of schools, a friendly high street and the cultural pull of the rugby stadium, and it is easy to see why families, professionals and downsizers alike set their sights here. For anyone relocating from further south, our team regularly handles moves between Sussex, Surrey and this part of London, and you can see the full picture of where we operate on our locations page.

The Feel of the Area: Riverside Living

The River Thames is the heart of Twickenham life. Riverside pubs, rowing clubs and gentle towpath walks give the area a slower, more pleasant rhythm than you might expect so close to the capital. Eel Pie Island, the curious little inhabited island in the river accessible by footbridge, adds a dash of bohemian charm and a famous musical heritage, opening its doors to visitors only occasionally.

Nearby, the riverside settings of Marble Hill Park and the open expanse of green space along the water give residents room to breathe. A short distance away lie some of London's grandest green spaces, including the vast Royal deer park at Richmond and the historic landscapes around Hampton, all within a comfortable outing. For a household used to the open countryside of West Sussex, Twickenham offers a reassuring amount of greenery for a London address.

Getting Around: Transport Links

Transport is one of Twickenham's strongest cards. Twickenham railway station runs frequent South Western Railway services into London Waterloo, with the journey typically taking around half an hour, which makes commuting into the capital very manageable. Several nearby stations add further options across the area, so wherever in Twickenham you settle, a train is rarely far.

By road, the A316 provides a direct route towards central London in one direction and out towards the M3 and the wider south-west in the other, which is handy for anyone keeping ties to Surrey or Hampshire. The proximity to the major motorway network is part of what makes Twickenham practical for those who travel for work. It also makes the area straightforward for a removals team to reach; our crews travel up from Sussex regularly, and good road access means a smoother, more predictable moving day. If you are planning the logistics, our house move planning guide sets out a sensible week-by-week timeline.

Cycling and Walking

For shorter journeys, Twickenham is wonderfully walkable and cyclable. The flat riverside paths make for pleasant, traffic-free cycling, and much of daily life, the shops, schools, the station, can be reached comfortably on foot. This is part of the appeal for families looking to swap a car-dependent existence for something gentler.

Schools and Family Life

Twickenham and the wider borough of Richmond upon Thames have a long-standing reputation for strong schools, which is a major draw for families. The area offers a good spread of well-regarded state primary and secondary schools, alongside a number of respected independent options, giving parents genuine choice. Demand for places at the most popular schools is naturally high, so it is worth researching catchment areas carefully before committing to a particular street.

Beyond formal schooling, Twickenham is a thoroughly family-friendly place. Parks, riverside playgrounds, swimming, sports clubs and the kind of safe, green streets where children can grow up with a bit of freedom all add to its appeal. The community feel is real; local events, markets and the rhythms of the rugby calendar give residents plenty to gather around. If you are moving with little ones, our advice on storage solutions for kids can help you settle a children's room quickly in the new home.

Shopping, Eating and Things to Do

Twickenham's town centre offers a satisfying mix of high-street names and genuinely good independents, with cafes, delis, restaurants and traditional pubs that give the place its character. Church Street, the pedestrianised heart of the old town, is particularly charming, lined with independent shops and eateries and host to regular markets and events.

For a wider shopping trip, the larger centres of neighbouring Kingston and Richmond are close at hand, adding department stores and bigger-name retail to the local independents. Culturally, Twickenham punches above its weight: the famous rugby stadium and its museum draw visitors from around the world, while riverside theatres, music venues and the area's musical heritage give residents plenty to enjoy without travelling far.

The Rugby, of Course

It would be remiss to write about Twickenham without mentioning the rugby. The stadium is the home of English rugby and a national institution, and on match days the whole area takes on a festive, slightly chaotic energy. For most residents this is a welcome part of the local identity, though it is worth being aware that match days bring crowds, road closures and busier trains. If you are planning a move, it is sensible to avoid a major fixture day, and a good removals firm will help you steer around it.

Green Spaces and the Outdoors

One of the quiet joys of living in Twickenham is how easy it is to be outdoors and among greenery. Beyond the riverside paths, the area sits within striking distance of some of London's most celebrated open spaces. The historic royal parks and the grand riverside gardens nearby offer everything from deer-grazed parkland to formal landscaped grounds, all close enough for a regular weekend walk or a midweek breath of fresh air.

Closer to home, Twickenham has its own generous scattering of local parks, greens and playing fields, the kind of everyday spaces where dogs are walked, children play and neighbours bump into one another. For anyone moving from the rolling countryside of West Sussex, this matters: the fear of losing all that green when relocating to London is largely unfounded here. Twickenham manages to feel surprisingly leafy for somewhere so well connected, and that combination is a big part of why people who move in tend to stay.

The River as a Lifestyle

The Thames is not just scenery in Twickenham; it is a way of life for many residents. Rowing and sailing clubs, riverside running and cycling, paddleboarding in the warmer months and simply sitting outside a waterside pub on a summer evening all form part of the local rhythm. Living near the river encourages an outdoor, active lifestyle in a way that more landlocked parts of London cannot quite match, and it gives the area a gentle, holiday-like quality that residents treasure year-round. Many newcomers say it is the river, more than anything else, that made them fall for the place, and it is easy to understand why once you have spent a golden evening watching the boats drift by on the water.

What Kind of Homes Will You Find?

Twickenham's housing is as varied as its residents. You will find handsome Victorian and Edwardian terraces, generous 1930s semis, riverside apartments and pockets of period cottages, alongside more modern developments. The closer to the river and the old town you get, the more characterful, and sought-after, the properties tend to be. As an established and desirable south-west London neighbourhood, Twickenham is not a budget area, but it offers genuine value in lifestyle for what you pay.

Whatever style of home you are moving into, a little forethought about access pays off. Period terraces can mean narrow staircases and tight parking; riverside flats may involve lifts, communal entrances or restricted loading times. Flagging these details to your removals team in advance lets them bring the right vehicle and plan the day properly. Our crews are well used to the access challenges of London streets, and handling delicate or valuable pieces is second nature, particularly given our specialism in white glove and high-value moves.

Planning Your Move to Twickenham

Relocating to Twickenham, whether from elsewhere in London, from the home counties or from further afield in Sussex, Surrey, Hampshire or Kent, follows the same principles as any well-run move: start early, declutter before you pack, and lean on experienced people for the heavy work. As a family-run firm established in 2016, we have built our reputation on calm, careful moves, and we are LAPADA members, Checkatrade-verified and fully covered with liability insurance up to £10m.

Choose the Right Service for the Size of Move

Not every move needs a full removals team. If you are relocating a flat, a single room or a handful of larger items into Twickenham, our man and van service, with rates from £80, is often the most cost-effective choice. For a whole house, our full house removals service handles everything from packing to placement in your new rooms. If you would like a clear sense of costs before committing, our pricing guide explains how removals are typically costed.

Mind the Match Days and the Parking

Two practical Twickenham-specific tips. First, check the rugby fixture calendar and avoid moving on a match day if you possibly can, as the crowds and road closures make access difficult. Second, sort your parking in advance: many Twickenham streets are permit-controlled, so arranging a temporary loading bay or permit with the council ensures the removals van can get close to your door and keeps the day moving.

Consider Storage for the Gap

Completion and move-in dates rarely align perfectly, and London property chains can be unpredictable. If there is a gap, or if you are downsizing into a smaller Twickenham home, our secure storage service bridges it neatly. You can estimate how much space you will need using our storage calculator, and store anything that does not fit straight away until you are ready for it.

Settling In: Your First Weeks in Twickenham

Once the van has gone and the boxes are in, the pleasure of Twickenham reveals itself in the everyday. Take a walk along the river in your first week, find your local cafe and pub, and get a feel for the rhythm of the place. Register with a local GP and dentist promptly, update your address details, and have a wander through the old town to orient yourself.

Twickenham rewards residents who get involved. The markets, the riverside events, the local clubs and the community spirit are all there to be enjoyed, and they are how a new house quickly becomes home. For a few more practical pointers on those early days, our five top tips for moving home are worth a read before the big day.

Is Twickenham Right for You?

Twickenham suits a particular kind of mover beautifully: someone who wants the connectivity and culture of London without surrendering green space, riverside calm and a genuine community feel. Families value the schools and the safe, leafy streets; professionals value the fast commute; downsizers value the walkability and the gentle pace. It is not the cheapest corner of the capital, but for what it offers in quality of life, many find it excellent value.

If a move to Twickenham is on your horizon, we would be glad to help you make it a smooth one. Whether you are coming up from the Sussex coast or relocating across London, you can request a friendly, no-obligation quote for your move online, or simply pick up the phone and talk it through with us on 01903 893731. We will help you choose the right service, plan around the match-day calendar, and get you settled into riverside life with the minimum of fuss.
faqs: How far is Twickenham from central London?

Twickenham sits roughly ten miles south-west of central London on the north bank of the Thames. Trains from Twickenham station into London Waterloo typically take around half an hour, and the A316 gives a direct road route towards the capital one way and the M3 the other, so it is well connected for commuters.
Is Twickenham a good place for families?

Very much so. The wider borough of Richmond upon Thames has a strong reputation for schools, and Twickenham offers parks, riverside playgrounds, sports clubs and safe, green streets. Demand for the most popular school places is high, so it is wise to research catchment areas carefully before settling on a particular street.
When should I avoid moving house in Twickenham?

Try to avoid rugby match days at the stadium, as they bring large crowds, road closures and much busier trains, all of which make access difficult for a removals van. Check the fixture calendar when choosing your date, and arrange parking or a loading bay in advance, since many Twickenham streets are permit-controlled.
What sort of homes are available in Twickenham?

A varied mix, including Victorian and Edwardian terraces, 1930s semis, riverside apartments, period cottages and modern developments. Properties nearer the river and the old town tend to be the most characterful and sought-after. It is an established, desirable area, so worth flagging access details like narrow stairs or communal entrances to your movers in advance.
Can you handle a move to Twickenham from Sussex?

Yes. As a Sussex-based family firm we regularly move households between Sussex, Surrey, Hampshire, Kent and this part of south-west London, with good motorway links making the journey straightforward. We can arrange anything from a man and van for smaller loads to a full house removal, plus storage to bridge any gap between dates.
